meta.name: FarmHelp prototype knowledge base
meta.note: This is a small demo dataset. Replace or extend with trusted local extension material for a real use case.

nutrient-or-water-stress.name: Nutrient or water-related stress (possible)
nutrient-or-water-stress.description: Leaf color change can come from nutrient imbalance, watering issues, or combined stress. Patterns matter.
inspect: Is yellowing more on older leaves, newer leaves, or both?
Are symptoms uniform across the field or patchy?
Check soil moisture and drainage in affected spots.
genericActions: Review watering schedule and avoid both over- and under-watering.
Consider a simple soil/nutrient check if available (local extension).
Ensure adequate plant spacing and sunlight exposure.
escalateIf: Rapid spreading across many plants, or severe loss of vigor.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

general-plant-stress.name: General plant stress (possible)
general-plant-stress.description: Stress signals often have multiple causes. Use observation to narrow what to check next.
inspect: Look for patterns: edges vs center, older vs newer leaves, and within-row vs between-row.
Note when symptoms started relative to weather changes or management actions.
genericActions: Focus on basic agronomic checks: irrigation, weed competition, and crop management basics.
Keep records for discussion with an agronomist.
escalateIf: You cannot find a simple agronomic explanation after basic checks.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

check-soil-and-drainage.name: Soil moisture and drainage check (possible)
check-soil-and-drainage.description: Poor drainage or inconsistent moisture can worsen many issues, including yellowing and growth loss.
inspect: After watering/rain, does water stay pooled or drain quickly?
Is the affected area low-lying or near compacted spots?
genericActions: Improve drainage where possible (field-level measures).
Adjust irrigation amounts/timing and monitor soil moisture.
escalateIf: Repeated waterlogging or rapid decline despite adjustments.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

leaf-spot-complex.name: Leaf spot complex (possible group)
leaf-spot-complex.description: Lesions can come from different causes (often fungal/bacterial or other plant health factors). Do not treat as a confirmed diagnosis.
inspect: Do spots have clear patterns (size/shape/color) and do they spread over time?
Check leaf undersides and compare healthy vs affected areas.
Note humidity conditions and whether overhead irrigation is used.
genericActions: Remove and destroy heavily affected leaves (where appropriate).
Improve airflow (spacing/weeding) and reduce leaf wetness.
Avoid splash water on leaves when possible.
escalateIf: Spreading quickly, severe defoliation, or repeated outbreaks year after year.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

humidity-and-splash-risk.name: Humidity and splash risk (possible driver)
humidity-and-splash-risk.description: Many leaf problems worsen when leaves stay wet and when water splashes between plants.
inspect: Is the problem worse in low-airflow areas or after frequent rains?
Does the irrigation method wet the foliage?
genericActions: Use irrigation practices that minimize leaf wetness.
Manage weeds to improve airflow.
escalateIf: Continued worsening despite reducing leaf wetness.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

sanitation-and-spread-control.name: Sanitation and spread control (possible steps)
sanitation-and-spread-control.description: Reducing spread can help limit damage while you consult experts.
inspect: Are lesions clustered in specific rows/areas?
Is there evidence of rapid spread after management events?
genericActions: Sanitize tools between plots if you move between affected and healthy areas.
Remove plant debris when appropriate.
escalateIf: Widespread spread with significant yield impact.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

water-and-root-stress.name: Water and root stress (possible)
water-and-root-stress.description: Wilting may come from moisture issues or root-related problems. Heat can also cause temporary wilting.
inspect: Wilting occurs at midday and improves in the evening, or it persists?
Check soil moisture at root depth.
If safe, inspect roots for rot or poor development.
genericActions: Adjust irrigation timing and amount; check drainage.
Avoid stressing the crop further while investigating.
Record weather conditions and changes to irrigation.
escalateIf: Persistent wilting that does not recover, or suspected root rot.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

heat-stress-and-irrigation.name: Heat stress and irrigation timing (possible driver)
heat-stress-and-irrigation.description: Hot weather can cause wilting even without disease. The pattern helps distinguish temporary vs persistent stress.
inspect: Are symptoms worst during peak heat hours?
Are affected plants near shade differences or irrigation differences?
genericActions: Consider irrigation timing that supports recovery (follow local best practice).
Use shade/mulch approaches where feasible.
escalateIf: Symptoms persist despite appropriate moisture management.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

check-for-root-problems.name: Root problem check (possible)
check-for-root-problems.description: If wilting is persistent, root health becomes a priority for investigation.
inspect: Look for root discoloration, poor root growth, or dead roots (if safe to inspect).
Assess whether plants are waterlogged or extremely dry.
genericActions: Fix drainage or irrigation issues where possible.
Consult an agronomist if root symptoms are severe.
escalateIf: Major root damage, rapid plant loss, or widespread persistent wilting.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

powdery-mildew-group.name: Powdery mildew group (possible)
powdery-mildew-group.description: Powdery-looking growth can indicate a mildew-type issue. Patterns and spread rate matter.
inspect: Is there a white powdery coating on leaf surfaces?
Does it spread under humid conditions or after canopy closure?
genericActions: Improve airflow (weed control and spacing).
Remove heavily affected leaves when appropriate.
Reduce humidity around the crop canopy.
escalateIf: Rapid spread or severe canopy coverage.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

airflow-and-humidity-management.name: Airflow and humidity management (possible driver)
airflow-and-humidity-management.description: Many leaf diseases/mildews are encouraged by poor airflow and persistent moisture/humidity.
inspect: Are plants crowded with low airflow?
Is the disease worse in shaded or dense parts?
genericActions: Improve airflow by managing weeds and canopy density.
Avoid creating unnecessary leaf wetness.
escalateIf: No improvement after basic management changes.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

early-action.name: Early action (helps reduce spread)
early-action.description: Acting early can reduce spread and preserve yield.
inspect: Are early symptoms limited to the first areas, or already widespread?
genericActions: Treat this as an early intervention moment: isolate affected areas where possible.
Keep notes for later comparison.
escalateIf: If spread becomes widespread within days.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

insect-feeding-damage.name: Insect feeding damage (possible)
insect-feeding-damage.description: Holes/chewed tissue often come from insects or other pests. Confirmation requires scouting.
inspect: Look for insects, larvae, or feeding signs on leaf undersides.
Check nearby plants and field edges.
genericActions: Scout at consistent times of day and record counts.
Use appropriate integrated pest management (IPM) methods recommended locally.
escalateIf: Severe defoliation or rapid pest increase.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

scouting-and-traps.name: Scouting and trapping (possible step)
scouting-and-traps.description: Systematic scouting helps confirm whether pests are involved.
inspect: Are there eggs or larvae on the underside of leaves?
Do you see recurring adult insects in the same areas?
genericActions: Set and monitor traps if you have access to locally recommended options.
Record the timing and location of observations.
escalateIf: If populations rise quickly and threaten yield.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

protect-and-prevent.name: Protect and prevent (possible strategies)
protect-and-prevent.description: Prevention and barriers can reduce re-infestation while you plan next steps.
inspect: Are there gaps in crop management or nearby weeds hosting pests?
genericActions: Maintain weed control and field sanitation.
Consider physical protections where suitable and safe.
escalateIf: If you cannot control pest pressure with basic scouting and preventive steps.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

stress-or-pest-associated-curl.name: Leaf curling causes (possible group)
stress-or-pest-associated-curl.description: Curling can be caused by stress or pests. Use observation to decide what to check next.
inspect: Is curling happening with discoloration, spots, or only deformation?
Check the leaf underside for small pests or eggs.
genericActions: Monitor irrigation and nutrient stress factors.
Use local IPM recommendations if pests are observed.
escalateIf: If plants fail to recover after basic stress management.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

underside-scouting.name: Underside scouting (possible step)
underside-scouting.description: Many pests hide on leaf undersides. Observations here help narrow causes.
inspect: Look for aphids, mites, or other small pests on the underside.
Look for sticky residue or honeydew if relevant.
genericActions: Remove lightly infested leaves (if appropriate) and monitor again.
Discuss confirmed pests with an agronomist if severe.
escalateIf: If curling spreads quickly or plants show major damage.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

environmental-drift-and-stress-check.name: Environmental stress check (possible driver)
environmental-drift-and-stress-check.description: Recent management changes, weather extremes, or chemical exposure can contribute to stress patterns.
inspect: Did symptoms start after a new input (fertilizer, pesticide, herbicide) or unusual weather?
Are symptoms uniform or localized to application areas?
genericActions: Avoid changing multiple variables at once; record what changed first.
Seek local expert advice if you suspect chemical/management-related stress.
escalateIf: If there is severe damage or uncertainty about recent inputs.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

growth-retardation-check.name: Growth retardation check (possible)
growth-retardation-check.description: Stunting can come from nutrients, soil conditions, planting issues, or early infestations.
inspect: Are plants evenly stunted or patchy?
Check root development and compare with nearby healthy plants.
genericActions: Review planting depth, spacing, and seed quality.
Consider soil testing if possible and follow local recommendations.
escalateIf: If many plants die or yield is clearly at risk.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

nutrition-soil-and-planting-quality.name: Nutrition/soil/planting quality (possible)
nutrition-soil-and-planting-quality.description: Early growth is sensitive to nutrients, moisture, and correct planting conditions.
inspect: Was the field previously cropped, and were residues managed?
Does stunting match low-fertility zones or drainage zones?
genericActions: Use appropriate nutrient management based on local advice.
Fix spacing/weed competition factors.
escalateIf: If stunting persists after basic agronomic corrections.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

early-infestation-or-conditions.name: Early infestation or condition mismatch (possible)
early-infestation-or-conditions.description: Early pest pressure or unsuitable conditions can limit growth.
inspect: Any insect signs during early stage?
Is the problem limited to certain rows?
genericActions: Scout regularly and adjust management only after observing patterns.
Consult local extension for crop- and region-specific advice.
escalateIf: If growth is severely reduced and spreading rapidly.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).

urgent-scouting.name: Urgent scouting (possible)
urgent-scouting.description: Early wilting patterns can be urgent; prioritize investigation and record-keeping.
inspect: Does wilting occur consistently, not just during peak heat?
Any signs of root discoloration or severe soil moisture issues?
genericActions: Check soil moisture and improve drainage/irrigation if needed.
Contact a local agronomist if unsure.
escalateIf: Rapid decline within days or suspected severe root problems.
sources: Local extension guidance (replace with your sources).
